steps.title: What Do You Like? 💛
steps.desc: Talk about hobbies and what you enjoy! Learn the important Dutch verbs for liking and doing activities.

steps.note: One of the most-used Dutch words! 'Leuk' = fun/nice/cool. 'Ik vind het leuk' = I like it (I find it nice).
Dutch way of saying 'I like': Ik vind [thing] leuk. Literally: 'I find football nice.'
Put 'graag' after the verb: Ik zwem graag = I like to swim. 'Graag gedaan!' = You're welcome!

steps.example: Ik sport drie keer per week.
steps.exampleEn: I exercise three times a week.
steps.note: 'Sporten' covers all exercise. 'Sport' as a noun = any sport.
'Ik lees' = I read. Past: 'Ik heb gelezen'. The Dutch are avid readers!
'Koken' = to cook. Also: to boil! 'Het water kookt' = the water is boiling.

steps.title: 💛 Two ways to say 'I like'
steps.text:
Dutch has two patterns:

1️⃣ Ik vind [thing] leuk. — I like [thing].
Ik vind voetbal leuk. — I like football.
Ik vind Nederland mooi. — I find NL beautiful.

2️⃣ Ik [verb] graag. — I like to [verb].
Ik lees graag. — I like to read.
Ik kook graag. — I like to cook.
Ik fiets graag. — I like to cycle.

❌ Ik hou van... = I love... (stronger!)
Ik hou van kaas. — I love cheese.
Ik hou van jou. — I love you.

steps.q: How do you say 'I like football'?

steps.title: Weekend Plans 🎬
steps.desc: What do you do on weekends? Movies, going out, sports — talk about leisure activities.

steps.note: 'Ik ga zwemmen' = I go swimming. 'Het zwembad' = the swimming pool.
'Wandelen' = leisurely walk/hike. 'Lopen' = regular walking. The Dutch love a 'boswandeling' (forest walk).

steps.example: Zullen we naar de film gaan?
steps.exampleEn: Shall we go to the movies?
steps.note: 'De bioscoop' = the cinema. 'Zullen we...?' = Shall we...? (great for suggesting plans!)

steps.exampleEn: Tonight we're going out to eat.
steps.note: Separable: 'We gaan uit eten' or 'We gaan vanavond uit eten.' Very common weekend activity.
Super useful! 'Zin hebben in' = to feel like/fancy. 'Ik heb zin in pizza!' = I feel like pizza!

steps.title: 🇳🇱 Dutch weekend culture
steps.text:
Typical Dutch weekend:

🛒 Zaterdag: boodschappen doen (groceries)
☕ Terrasje pakken (sit at a café terrace)
🚲 Fietsen in de natuur (cycling in nature)
👨‍👩‍👧 Bij familie op bezoek (visiting family)
⚽ Voetbal kijken (watching football)
🎂 Verjaardagen (birthday parties — huge in NL!)

Suggesting plans:
• Zullen we...? — Shall we...?
• Heb je zin in...? — Do you feel like...?
• Zin in een biertje? — Fancy a beer?

steps.title: Gefeliciteerd! 🎂
steps.desc: Dutch celebrations are unique — especially birthdays! Learn the social vocabulary for parties and festivities.

steps.note: A de-word. Literally 'year-day'. Birthdays are a BIG deal in Dutch culture.
The most important celebration word. You say it to EVERYONE — the birthday person AND their family!

steps.exampleEn: Are you coming to my party?
steps.note: A het-word! Related to English 'feast'. 'Feestje' (diminutive) = smaller/casual party.
A het-word. From French. Also: 'cadeautje' (small gift, diminutive). 'Sinterklaas cadeautjes' are a huge tradition!
A de-word. On your birthday in NL, YOU bring the cake — to work, to school, to family. Not the other way around!
The Dutch toast. Always make EYE CONTACT when clinking glasses — it's considered bad luck otherwise!

steps.title: 🎂 Dutch birthday rules
steps.text:
Dutch birthdays are VERY different:

1️⃣ YOU bring cake to work/school (not your colleagues)
2️⃣ You congratulate EVERYONE at the party — not just the birthday person!
   'Gefeliciteerd met je moeder!' = Congrats with your mother!
3️⃣ The 'verjaardagskring' (birthday circle): everyone sits in a circle, drinks coffee, eats cake
4️⃣ 'Abraham/Sarah': turning 50 is huge — you've 'seen Abraham (or Sarah)'
5️⃣ The birthday calendar hangs in the TOILET (yes, really!) so nobody forgets a birthday

Foreigners find the verjaardagskring hilarious but it's deeply Dutch. Embrace it!

steps.q: On your birthday in the Netherlands, who brings cake?
